The patch was released two days before the flight and features an F-15, symbolizing Hendren's F-15 Eagle, two Diamond DA42s, symbolizing Gutiérrez's and Drexler's love of flying, people in the windows to symbolize the crew, the Kármán line to represent Drexler's ambition for space, a beacon of light to represent Fernandez's motto, and a stethoscope and female gender symbol to represent Edwards's medical career.

Michaela “Michi” Benthaus celebrates after going to space on Blue Origin’s NS-37 suborbital flight Dec. 20. WASHINGTON — Blue Origin flew its final New Shepard suborbital mission of the year Dec. 20, carrying six people, including the first person who uses a wheelchair to travel to space. She is the first wheelchair user to fly to space. Her flight was arranged by another person on the flight, Hans Koenigsmann, one of the first employees of SpaceX who spent two decades at the company. Benthaus said in the video that she felt a responsibility as the first wheelchair user to fly to space. “NS-37 marks our ninth flight of the year and sets the stage for an increase in New Shepard flight rate in 2026 and beyond,” Phil Joyce, senior vice president for New Shepard at Blue Origin, said in a post-flight statement.","score":0.62906903,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.blueorigin.com/missions/by/human-flight","title":"Human Flight | Missions | Blue Origin","content":"Blue Origin successfully completed the 38th flight for the New Shepard program and first of 2026 on January 22.
